verses.commentary: Nicodemus, a Pharisee and member of the Jewish ruling council. Religious elite. Unlike his peers, he seeks Jesus. Authority meets greater authority.
He came at night. Fear or contemplation? Rabbi, we know you are from God. Respectful but incomplete understanding. Signs prove divine presence but not full identity.
You must be born again. Anōthen (ἄνωθεν), from above or again. Jesus redirects from signs to necessity. Kingdom entrance requires radical regeneration, not incremental improvement.
How can someone be born when old? Nicodemus thinks physically. The spiritual teacher misses spiritual truth. Human wisdom cannot grasp divine reality.
Born of water and the Spirit. Water may reference natural birth or John's baptism. Spirit is essential. Entrance to the kingdom is God's work, not human achievement.
Flesh gives birth to flesh. Like produces like. Spirit gives birth to spirit. Categories do not mix. Spiritual life requires spiritual generation from above.
Do not be amazed. You must be born again. Universal requirement, not exception. The plural you: all people need new birth, even religious leaders.
The wind (pneuma, πνεῦμα, also Spirit) blows where it pleases. Sovereign, unpredictable, invisible yet real. So it is with the Spirit's regeneration. Divine freedom, not human control.
How can this be? Nicodemus still baffled. The mystery of regeneration confounds human logic. Spiritual reality transcends natural understanding.
You are Israel's teacher. Irony. Ezekiel 36 promised new heart and Spirit. Nicodemus should have known. Old Testament prepares for new birth; rabbis missed it.
We speak of what we know. Jesus and the Father. You people do not accept our testimony. Religious leaders reject divine revelation despite evidence.
Earthly things versus heavenly things. If new birth is incomprehensible, how will deeper mysteries be understood? Failure at the foundational level predicts failure at advanced truths.
No one has ascended to heaven except the Son of Man who came from heaven. Unique access. Jesus alone bridges heaven and earth. He reveals what He has seen.

As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness (Numbers 21:4-9), so the Son of Man must be lifted up. The bronze serpent brought healing through looking in faith. The cross will bring salvation the same way.
Looking to Jesus lifted up saves. Simple, scandalous, sufficient.

God so loved the world. Cosmic scope. He gave His one and only Son. Costliest gift. That whoever believes shall not perish but have eternal life. Universal offer, particular response. Love, gift, faith, life.

God did not send the Son to condemn but to save. Mission is rescue, not judgment. The world's condemnation is self-inflicted through unbelief. Jesus came as Savior, not judge, though rejection brings judgment.
Whoever believes is not condemned. Present tense: already secure. Whoever does not believe stands condemned already. The verdict precedes final judgment. Unbelief seals condemnation.
This is the verdict: Light has come, but people loved darkness. Moral explanation for unbelief. Not lack of evidence but love of evil. Preference, not ignorance, drives rejection.
Everyone who does evil hates the light. Will not come to the light. Fear of exposure keeps people in darkness. Sin avoids the light that would reveal and heal it.
Whoever lives by the truth comes into the light. Truth lovers seek exposure. They want deeds seen as done in God. Transparency marks those who trust God's verdict over self-deception.
Jesus and disciples went into the Judean countryside. He spent time with them and baptized. Intimate discipleship. Teaching through proximity. Ministry continues beyond dramatic encounters.
John also was baptizing at Aenon near Salim. Plenty of water. Both ministries overlap geographically and temporally. Transition period. Old and new coexist briefly.
The forerunner's freedom and ministry are ending. The main event is beginning.
An argument about ceremonial washing. Disputes over purity. Jewish concerns about ritual cleanliness create friction. The debate reveals deeper issues of authority and identity.
John's disciples report to him: That man you testified about is baptizing, and everyone is going to Him. Anxiety. Fear of being eclipsed. Comparison breeds insecurity.
A person can receive only what is given from heaven. John grounds identity in divine calling, not popular acclaim. Roles are assigned by God, not chosen by ambition.
I am not the Messiah but am sent ahead of Him. Clarity of role. John knows his identity and mission. He is forerunner, not fulfillment.

The bride belongs to the bridegroom. The friend rejoices at the bridegroom's voice. That joy is mine, and it is now complete. Wedding imagery. John's role is to present the bride to Jesus, not compete for her. Success is Jesus' increase.
Decreasing self-importance, increasing Christ-exaltation. John models the trajectory of all true ministry.
The one from above is above all. The one from earth speaks as one from earth. Origin determines authority. Jesus comes from heaven; earthly voices cannot compare. Divine versus human perspective.
Tragic irony. The truth from heaven is rejected by earth.
Rejection impugns God's integrity. Faith validates divine testimony.
The one God has sent speaks the words of God. Jesus is God's perfect spokesman. God gives the Spirit without limit. Full divine empowerment, unrestricted anointing.
The Father loves the Son. Eternal relationship. Has placed everything in His hands. Total authority delegated. Creation, salvation, judgment all belong to Jesus.

Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life. Present possession, not future hope. Whoever rejects the Son will not see life. God's wrath remains on them. Stark binary. Jesus is the only way; rejection leaves one under divine judgment.
